probe_designs.content: Compute the positive-class prevalence for each of the 40 CelebA attributes in the training set. CelebA is notoriously imbalanced (e.g., 'Bald' ~2%, 'Mustache' ~4%, 'Young' ~77%, 'Attractive' ~50%). Plot a histogram of positive rates and flag attributes with prevalence <5% or >95%. Healthy signal: training loss weighting (e.g., pos_weight in BCEWithLogitsLoss) or sampling compensates for imbalance, and per-attribute recall on rare classes is non-trivial (>20%). Unhealthy: rare attributes collapse to always-negative predictions, yielding high accuracy but near-zero recall/F1.
possible_sources: Liu et al., 'Deep Learning Face Attributes in the Wild' (ICCV 2015)
He et al., 'Learning from Imbalanced Data' (TKDE 2009)
Cui et al., 'Class-Balanced Loss Based on Effective Number of Samples' (CVPR 2019)

probe_designs.content: Partition the test set by the 'Male' attribute (and optionally 'Pale_Skin' as a proxy for skin tone) and compute per-attribute accuracy, TPR, FPR, and F1 for each subgroup. Compute equalized-odds gaps (|TPR_male - TPR_female|, |FPR_male - FPR_female|) per attribute. Healthy: gaps <5% across most attributes. Unhealthy: large gaps (e.g., 'Attractive' or 'Young' showing >15% TPR gap across gender), indicating the model encodes demographic bias—a well-documented issue on CelebA.
possible_sources: Buolamwini & Gebru, 'Gender Shades' (FAT* 2018)
Hardt et al., 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' (NeurIPS 2016)
Ramaswamy et al., 'Fair Attribute Classification through Latent Space De-biasing' (CVPR 2021)

probe_designs.content: After sigmoid, bin each attribute's predicted probabilities into 15 equal-width bins and compute ECE = Σ (|bin|/N) · |acc(bin) - conf(bin)|, separately for each of the 40 attributes. Also produce reliability diagrams. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 for most attributes, curves close to diagonal. Unhealthy: systematic over-confidence (curve below diagonal) on rare attributes, indicating BCE-trained multi-label heads require temperature scaling or focal-loss retraining.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Naeini et al., 'Obtaining Well Calibrated Probabilities Using Bayesian Binning' (AAAI 2015)
Kull et al., 'Beyond Temperature Scaling' (NeurIPS 2019)

probe_designs.content: Track per-epoch mean BCE loss and mean attribute accuracy on train vs. CelebA official val and test splits. Compute the train–val gap at best-val-epoch. Healthy: smooth monotonically decreasing val loss reaching plateau; train–val accuracy gap <2–3%. Unhealthy: val loss diverges while train loss keeps decreasing (classic overfitting), or best-val-epoch is at epoch 1 (underfitting / optimizer issue). Also verify early-stopping was triggered by val, not test.
possible_sources: Prechelt, 'Early Stopping - But When?' (Neural Networks: Tricks of the Trade 1998)
Goodfellow et al., 'Deep Learning' textbook Ch. 7
timm training recipes (Wightman, 2021)

probe_designs.content: Evaluate the model on face images corrupted with Gaussian noise, motion blur, JPEG compression, brightness/contrast shifts, and occlusion patches at 5 severity levels (ImageNet-C protocol adapted for faces). Report mean F1 degradation relative to clean test. Healthy: graceful degradation (<10% mean-F1 drop at severity 3). Unhealthy: catastrophic collapse on blur/noise, indicating the model relies on high-frequency texture rather than semantic structure.
possible_sources: Hendrycks & Dietterich, 'Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions' (ICLR 2019)
Geirhos et al., 'ImageNet-trained CNNs are biased towards texture' (ICLR 2019)
Karkkainen & Joo, 'FairFace' (WACV 2021)

probe_designs.content: Compute the pairwise Pearson/Phi correlation matrix of the 40 ground-truth attributes in train set, then compute the model's prediction correlation matrix on test. Flag attribute pairs where predicted correlation >> label correlation (e.g., 'Wearing_Lipstick' ↔ 'Male' negative correlation amplified, or 'Heavy_Makeup' predicted from 'Female' cue). Evaluate on counterfactual/mismatched samples (e.g., males with lipstick). Healthy: correlation amplification factor near 1.0. Unhealthy: factor >1.5 indicates shortcut learning.
possible_sources: Zhao et al., 'Men Also Like Shopping: Reducing Gender Bias Amplification' (EMNLP 2017)
Sagawa et al., 'Distributionally Robust Neural Networks' / Group DRO (ICLR 2020)
Singh et al., 'Don't Judge an Object by Its Context' (CVPR 2020)

probe_designs.content: For each attribute, generate Grad-CAM/Score-CAM saliency maps on 50 positive test samples and check whether the heatmap aligns with the expected facial region (e.g., 'Eyeglasses' → eye region, 'Smiling' → mouth, 'Wearing_Hat' → top of head). Quantify via pointing-game accuracy using face-parsing segmentation masks (BiSeNet on CelebAMask-HQ). Healthy: >70% pointing-game accuracy for localized attributes. Unhealthy: saliency concentrated on background or unrelated regions, indicating the model uses global image statistics.
possible_sources: Selvaraju et al., 'Grad-CAM' (ICCV 2017)
Lee et al., 'CelebAMask-HQ' (CVPR 2020)
Zhang et al., 'Top-down Neural Attention by Excitation Backprop' (IJCV 2018)

probe_designs.probe_name: Per-Attribute F1 / mAP vs. Naïve Accuracy
probe_designs.content: Beyond reporting the CelebA-standard mean accuracy (~91% baseline), compute per-attribute precision, recall, F1, and mean average precision (mAP) at threshold 0.5 and at the threshold that maximizes per-attribute F1. Compare against the 'predict-majority-class' baseline per attribute. Healthy: mean F1 > 0.70 and every attribute beats its majority baseline by >5 F1 points. Unhealthy: attributes where F1≈0 while accuracy is high (e.g., 'Bald' at 98% accuracy but 0.1 F1), revealing metric-driven illusion of quality.
possible_sources: Liu et al., 'Deep Learning Face Attributes in the Wild' (ICCV 2015)
Hand & Chellappa, 'Attributes for Improved Attributes' (AAAI 2017)
He et al., 'Harnessing Synthesized Abstraction Images to Improve Facial Attribute Recognition' (IJCAI 2018)

probe_designs.content: Log wall-clock time per epoch, GPU memory, throughput (images/sec), and plot validation mean-F1 vs. wall-clock / vs. FLOPs. Compare the chosen timm backbone's efficiency frontier against lighter alternatives (MobileNetV3, EfficientNet-B0, ResNet-50). Healthy: validation F1 plateaus well before final epoch, and chosen model sits on or near the Pareto frontier. Unhealthy: still-rising val curve at training end (under-trained) or model is Pareto-dominated by a smaller backbone, suggesting over-parameterization for the task.
possible_sources: Tan & Le, 'EfficientNet' (ICML 2019)
Dehghani et al., 'The Efficiency Misnomer' (ICLR 2022)
Wightman et al., 'ResNet Strikes Back' (NeurIPS Workshop 2021)

probe_designs.content: CelebA labels are known noisy (annotator disagreement on subjective attributes like 'Attractive', 'Young', 'Oval_Face'). Use confident-learning (cleanlab) to rank suspected mislabeled samples by predicted-probability vs. label disagreement, and manually inspect top-100. Also compute per-attribute loss distribution and flag samples with consistently high loss across epochs (possible mislabels or outliers). Healthy: high-loss samples are genuinely ambiguous edge cases (<3% estimated noise rate for objective attributes like 'Eyeglasses'). Unhealthy: subjective attributes show >15% estimated noise and the model over-fits these noisy labels, hurting generalization.
possible_sources: Northcutt et al., 'Confident Learning' (JAIR 2021)
Lingenfelter et al., 'A Quantitative Analysis of Labeling Issues in the CelebA Dataset' (ISVC 2022)
Song et al., 'Learning from Noisy Labels with Deep Neural Networks: A Survey' (TNNLS 2022)
